student_request:
Predictive modelling is based on the assumption that data that describes past events can be used to predict potential future behavior (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). In education, this technique has been commonly applied to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. This is done by using past data of student behavior and drop-out to learn the relationship between how students behaved before they dropped out. This information about how students behaved is then used in models to predict drop-out from student behavior. This prediction takes place using new data, not the data from which the model inferred the relationship between student behavior and drop out has been learn from (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). Inferences from the data as described above make it possible for an early intervention before the student may be at risk.
Central to such interventions is the idea of a student profile, i.e. a data-based description of student characteristics, needs or preferences. Such a profile can be used to tailor student support to each specific student, such as recommend learning materials that suit their specific needs or adapt existing learning materials to match their specific preferences. The goal of interventions based on student profile is to improve learning, retention and graduation rates (Baker et al., 2023; Brooks & Greer, 2014). Research also shows that predictive models can support educators by making complex data easier to interpret and act on. For example, results can be translated into learner personas – short, story-like descriptions of typical students – that help advisors and instructional designers plan targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring or online resources. These insights may also inform the redesign of teaching practices and guide how support services are allocated (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
Beyond individual classrooms, predictive modelling can also inform decision-making at the university level. When data from several courses or different cohorts of students are examined together, researchers may find patterns that may otherwise be missed. For instance, one empirical study found that biology students were most likely to drop out later in the course, around the sixth week of the semester (Gašević et al., 2016). In contrast, mathematics students tended to struggle earlier, probably because the material became more difficult sooner (Gašević et al., 2016). If the two cohorts were to be examined together without considering them as two different groups, the pattern would be more generic. Such pattern would capture the average, which here would describe neither biology students nor mathematics students. Specific findings like these can help teachers redesign courses, adjust teaching timelines, or add feedback opportunities at critical points. At the same time, administrators can use the information to decide where support services, such as tutoring or writing centers, are most needed (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Of course, predictions can be unfair when a model only has information that does not describe everyone correctly, for instance, if it only describes one behavior of one group of students. In these cases, the results of predictive modelling may be misleading. Predictions are also fairer when models are built with information that describes different groups of students. Using varied student information and checking the results regularly helps make predictions fairer (Kolkman, 2020). Accuracy of predictions also improves when models consider the course conditions in which students learn. Including factors such as whether a course design encourages self-regulated learning – so student behavior would include setting goals, monitoring progress, and reflecting on study strategies – produces results that are more meaningful and sensitive to educational course context (Gašević et al., 2016). In addition to supporting teachers and students, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress. When data from different studies are shared and compared, findings about student success and risk factors can be confirmed, building more reliable knowledge (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
At the institutional level, these models also contribute to ongoing improvement, as predictions are updated with new information from new students and used to guide decisions about courses and student support over time (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). It is important to acknowledge, however, that predictive modelling is not free of ethical risks. Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling is not just a technological tool. When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014). summarize the text
llm_response: Predictive modelling in education uses past data to predict future student behavior, such as drop-out rates. This technique creates student profiles to tailor interventions and support, improving learning outcomes. Predictive models can also inform decision-making at the university level, identifying patterns in student behavior across different courses. Fairness in predictions is ensured by using varied student information and considering course conditions. Predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress and ongoing improvement in education. However, ethical risks must be considered, such as reflecting inequalities or reducing student autonomy. When developed responsibly, predictive modelling can make education fairer and more responsive to student and teacher needs.

Predictive modelling is a powerful technique that uses historical data to predict future outcomes or behaviors. In the field of education, predictive modelling has emerged as a valuable tool to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. By analyzing past student behavior and dropout rates, predictive models can help educators intervene early and provide targeted support to improve student retention and graduation rates.

Beyond education, predictive modelling has a wide range of real-time applications in various industries. For example, in finance, predictive models are used to forecast stock prices and detect fraudulent transactions. In healthcare, predictive modelling can help predict patient outcomes and identify individuals at risk of certain diseases. In marketing, predictive models are used to personalize customer experiences and optimize advertising campaigns.

Overall, predictive modelling offers valuable insights and predictions that can drive informed decision-making and improve outcomes in real-time across different sectors.

student_request:
Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020). Now for this text, writme an introduction , two main body part, and conclusion
llm_response:
Introduction:
Predictive modelling, a technique that uses existing data to predict future events, has become increasingly prevalent in the field of education. While it offers the potential to enhance student support and improve outcomes, the application of predictive models in education also raises ethical concerns and risks. This essay will explore the benefits and challenges associated with predictive modelling in education, focusing on how these models can impact student classification, institutional decision-making, and the overall learning process.

Main Body Part 1:
One of the primary challenges of predictive modelling in education is the risk of reinforcing stereotypes and biases. Models that heavily rely on demographic data, such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status, may oversimplify students and define them based on their background rather than their potential. Additionally, the interpretation of student behavior data, such as online activity, can vary significantly depending on the context. For instance, the same action may indicate success in one course but failure in another due to differences in instructional design and course conditions. Relying solely on surface-level data like clicks or views may lead to misclassifying students and misunderstanding their engagement with course content.

Main Body Part 2:
At the institutional level, predictive models face limitations when applied broadly across different subjects. Predictors that are effective in one course may not hold the same meaning in another, leading to misinterpretations and ineffective interventions. The lack of theoretical foundations in many predictive models further complicates their reliability and interpretability. Accountability is another critical concern, as unfair outcomes produced by predictive models can harm vulnerable students and erode trust in the education system. The recent UK A-level grading controversy serves as a stark example of how automated systems can produce unjust results, highlighting the importance of clear rules and responsibility in predictive modelling.

Conclusion:
While predictive modelling in education offers potential benefits in improving student support and decision-making, its ethical risks and limitations must be carefully considered. The reliance on historical and demographic data can perpetuate inequalities and privacy concerns, while the lack of transparency and accountability can lead to unfair outcomes. Responsible use of predictive modelling is essential to mitigate these risks and ensure that these tools contribute positively to education without reinforcing existing disparities.

Important: Do not click “Next” until you finish writing your synthesis on THAII. If you leave the page, you will not be able to return. After submitting your synthesis in THAII and once the experimenter confirms, you may proceed to the next page of the survey.

Text A
Predictive modelling is based on the assumption that data that describes past events can be used to predict potential future behavior (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). In education, this technique has been commonly applied to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. This is done by using past data of student behavior and drop-out to learn the relationship between how students behaved before they dropped out. This information about how students behaved is then used in models to predict drop-out from student behavior. This prediction takes place using new data, not the data from which the model inferred the relationship between student behavior and drop out has been learn from (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). Inferences from the data as described above make it possible for an early intervention before the student may be at risk.
Central to such interventions is the idea of a student profile, i.e. a data-based description of student characteristics, needs or preferences. Such a profile can be used to tailor student support to each specific student, such as recommend learning materials that suit their specific needs or adapt existing learning materials to match their specific preferences. The goal of interventions based on student profile is to improve learning, retention and graduation rates (Baker et al., 2023; Brooks & Greer, 2014). Research also shows that predictive models can support educators by making complex data easier to interpret and act on. For example, results can be translated into learner personas – short, story-like descriptions of typical students – that help advisors and instructional designers plan targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring or online resources. These insights may also inform the redesign of teaching practices and guide how support services are allocated (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
Beyond individual classrooms, predictive modelling can also inform decision-making at the university level. When data from several courses or different cohorts of students are examined together, researchers may find patterns that may otherwise be missed. For instance, one empirical study found that biology students were most likely to drop out later in the course, around the sixth week of the semester (Gašević et al., 2016). In contrast, mathematics students tended to struggle earlier, probably because the material became more difficult sooner (Gašević et al., 2016). If the two cohorts were to be examined together without considering them as two different groups, the pattern would be more generic. Such pattern would capture the average, which here would describe neither biology students nor mathematics students. Specific findings like these can help teachers redesign courses, adjust teaching timelines, or add feedback opportunities at critical points. At the same time, administrators can use the information to decide where support services, such as tutoring or writing centers, are most needed (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Of course, predictions can be unfair when a model only has information that does not describe everyone correctly, for instance, if it only describes one behavior of one group of students. In these cases, the results of predictive modelling may be misleading. Predictions are also fairer when models are built with information that describes different groups of students. Using varied student information and checking the results regularly helps make predictions fairer (Kolkman, 2020). Accuracy of predictions also improves when models consider the course conditions in which students learn. Including factors such as whether a course design encourages self-regulated learning – so student behavior would include setting goals, monitoring progress, and reflecting on study strategies – produces results that are more meaningful and sensitive to educational course context (Gašević et al., 2016). In addition to supporting teachers and students, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress. When data from different studies are shared and compared, findings about student success and risk factors can be confirmed, building more reliable knowledge (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
At the institutional level, these models also contribute to ongoing improvement, as predictions are updated with new information from new students and used to guide decisions about courses and student support over time (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). It is important to acknowledge, however, that predictive modelling is not free of ethical risks. Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling is not just a technological tool. When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

Text B
Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).
llm_response:
The synthesis of Text A and Text B highlights the benefits and risks associated with predictive modelling in education. Predictive modelling utilizes past data to predict future student behavior, allowing for early intervention and tailored support to improve learning outcomes and retention rates. By analyzing data from various courses and student cohorts, predictive models can identify patterns that inform course redesign and allocation of support services. Additionally, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress by confirming findings about student success and risk factors.

However, the synthesis also acknowledges the ethical risks of predictive modelling. Models that rely heavily on demographic data or online activity may reinforce stereotypes and oversimplify student characteristics. The context in which data is collected and interpreted is crucial, as the same behavior may have different meanings in different courses. Predictive models may face limitations when applied broadly across all subjects, as predictors may not be universal and may not accurately reflect student needs in each context. Lack of transparency in how models work, accountability for unfair results, and potential privacy concerns are also highlighted as challenges in predictive modelling.

Overall, while predictive modelling can offer valuable insights and support in education, it is essential to use it responsibly, considering diverse student information, course context, and ethical implications. By addressing these challenges and limitations, predictive modelling can be a powerful tool to enhance educational practices and student outcomes.

Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020). help me summarize this article
llm_response:
Predictive modelling in education involves using past data to predict future student behavior and outcomes, with the goal of identifying at-risk students and implementing early interventions. These interventions are based on student profiles that describe individual characteristics and needs, aiming to improve learning outcomes and retention rates. Predictive models can also support educators by providing insights into student behavior and informing targeted interventions to enhance teaching practices.

However, there are ethical risks associated with predictive modelling in education. Models that rely heavily on demographic data may reinforce stereotypes and oversimplify students, potentially leading to misclassification. Additionally, the interpretation of student online activity data can vary depending on the context, and relying solely on surface-level behaviors may not accurately reflect student engagement with course content.

At the institutional level, predictive models may face limitations when applied broadly across different subjects, as predictors may have varying meanings in different contexts. Using the same model for all students can lead to misinterpretations and ineffective interventions that do not address the specific needs of learners and teachers. Furthermore, challenges arise when models lack theoretical foundations and accountability mechanisms, potentially resulting in unfair outcomes and eroding trust in education.

Overall, while predictive modelling offers benefits in improving educational processes and supporting decision-making, it is crucial to address ethical concerns and ensure responsible use of these models to avoid reinforcing existing inequalities and maintaining transparency and fairness in education. By considering diverse student information, regularly checking results, and incorporating feedback mechanisms, predictive modelling can be a valuable tool for enhancing educational outcomes while mitigating potential risks.

student_request: what can predictive models do to support students or educators
llm_response:
Predictive models can offer valuable support to both students and educators in various ways:

1. Early Identification of At-Risk Students: Predictive models can analyze past data to identify students who may be at risk of academic failure or dropping out. This early identification allows for timely interventions to support these students and improve their chances of success.

2. Personalized Learning: By creating student profiles based on individual characteristics and needs, predictive models can tailor learning materials and recommendations to match each student's specific requirements. This personalized approach can enhance student engagement and learning outcomes.

3. Targeted Interventions: Predictive models can provide insights into student behavior patterns, enabling educators to design targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring, or online resources to address specific needs and challenges faced by students.

4. Course Redesign and Teaching Practices: By analyzing data from different courses and student cohorts, predictive models can help educators identify patterns and make informed decisions about course redesign, adjustments in teaching timelines, and allocation of support services to enhance the overall learning experience.

5. Transparent and Easy Interpretation of Data: Predictive models can simplify complex data and make it easier for educators to interpret and act upon. By translating results into learner personas or short descriptions of typical students, educators can better understand student needs and plan effective interventions.

6. Scientific Progress and Knowledge Building: Predictive models contribute to scientific progress by sharing and comparing data from different studies, confirming findings about student success factors, and building more reliable knowledge in the field of education.

student_request: At the institutional level, what can predictive modelling do

At the institutional level, predictive modelling can provide valuable insights and support in various ways:

student_request: what are Limitations in predictive modeling
llm_response:
Limitations in predictive modeling in the context of education include:

1. **Stereotyping and Bias**: Predictive models may inadvertently reinforce stereotypes or biases if they rely heavily on demographic data. This can lead to misclassification of students based on factors such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status.

2. **Contextual Interpretation**: The interpretation of student online activity data can vary depending on the context. The same behavior may have different meanings in different courses or instructional settings, leading to potential misinterpretations.

3. **Subject-Specific Predictors**: Predictive models may face limitations when applied broadly across different subjects. Predictors that are effective in one subject may not hold the same meaning or predictive power in another, leading to challenges in generalizing predictions.

4. **Lack of Theoretical Foundations**: Some predictive models may lack a strong theoretical foundation, particularly in understanding how learning actually occurs in the classroom. This can limit the reliability and interpretability of the model's findings.

5. **Accountability and Fairness**: There may be challenges in ensuring accountability and fairness in predictive modeling outcomes. If a model produces unfair results, it may be unclear who is responsible for addressing these issues and ensuring equitable outcomes.

6. **Privacy Concerns**: Collecting and analyzing large amounts of data for predictive modeling raises privacy concerns, as students may feel that their actions and behaviors are being closely monitored and analyzed.

7. **Hidden Failures**: Failures in predictive modeling, particularly those affecting smaller or less visible student groups, may remain hidden without proper checks and reviews. This can erode trust in the predictive modeling process over time.

8. **Inequality Reproduction**: Predictive models that rely on historical and demographic data may inadvertently reproduce and amplify existing inequalities in the education system, rather than addressing or mitigating them.

Addressing these limitations requires careful consideration of ethical implications, transparency in model workings, inclusion of diverse student information, regular checks and reviews of model outcomes, and a commitment to fairness and accountability in the predictive modeling process.

student_request:
"Text A
Predictive modelling is based on the assumption that data that describes past events can be used to predict potential future behavior (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). In education, this technique has been commonly applied to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. This is done by using past data of student behavior and drop-out to learn the relationship between how students behaved before they dropped out. This information about how students behaved is then used in models to predict drop-out from student behavior. This prediction takes place using new data, not the data from which the model inferred the relationship between student behavior and drop out has been learn from (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). Inferences from the data as described above make it possible for an early intervention before the student may be at risk.
Central to such interventions is the idea of a student profile, i.e. a data-based description of student characteristics, needs or preferences. Such a profile can be used to tailor student support to each specific student, such as recommend learning materials that suit their specific needs or adapt existing learning materials to match their specific preferences. The goal of interventions based on student profile is to improve learning, retention and graduation rates (Baker et al., 2023; Brooks & Greer, 2014). Research also shows that predictive models can support educators by making complex data easier to interpret and act on. For example, results can be translated into learner personas – short, story-like descriptions of typical students – that help advisors and instructional designers plan targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring or online resources. These insights may also inform the redesign of teaching practices and guide how support services are allocated (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
Beyond individual classrooms, predictive modelling can also inform decision-making at the university level. When data from several courses or different cohorts of students are examined together, researchers may find patterns that may otherwise be missed. For instance, one empirical study found that biology students were most likely to drop out later in the course, around the sixth week of the semester (Gašević et al., 2016). In contrast, mathematics students tended to struggle earlier, probably because the material became more difficult sooner (Gašević et al., 2016). If the two cohorts were to be examined together without considering them as two different groups, the pattern would be more generic. Such pattern would capture the average, which here would describe neither biology students nor mathematics students. Specific findings like these can help teachers redesign courses, adjust teaching timelines, or add feedback opportunities at critical points. At the same time, administrators can use the information to decide where support services, such as tutoring or writing centers, are most needed (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Of course, predictions can be unfair when a model only has information that does not describe everyone correctly, for instance, if it only describes one behavior of one group of students. In these cases, the results of predictive modelling may be misleading. Predictions are also fairer when models are built with information that describes different groups of students. Using varied student information and checking the results regularly helps make predictions fairer (Kolkman, 2020). Accuracy of predictions also improves when models consider the course conditions in which students learn. Including factors such as whether a course design encourages self-regulated learning – so student behavior would include setting goals, monitoring progress, and reflecting on study strategies – produces results that are more meaningful and sensitive to educational course context (Gašević et al., 2016). In addition to supporting teachers and students, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress. When data from different studies are shared and compared, findings about student success and risk factors can be confirmed, building more reliable knowledge (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
At the institutional level, these models also contribute to ongoing improvement, as predictions are updated with new information from new students and used to guide decisions about courses and student support over time (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). It is important to acknowledge, however, that predictive modelling is not free of ethical risks. Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling is not just a technological tool. When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014)." can you make a summary of this text about predictive modelling? which key points does it refer to? write it with bullet points

student_request:
Text A
Predictive modelling is based on the assumption that data that describes past events can be used to predict potential future behavior (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). In education, this technique has been commonly applied to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. This is done by using past data of student behavior and drop-out to learn the relationship between how students behaved before they dropped out. This information about how students behaved is then used in models to predict drop-out from student behavior. This prediction takes place using new data, not the data from which the model inferred the relationship between student behavior and drop out has been learn from (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). Inferences from the data as described above make it possible for an early intervention before the student may be at risk.
Central to such interventions is the idea of a student profile, i.e. a data-based description of student characteristics, needs or preferences. Such a profile can be used to tailor student support to each specific student, such as recommend learning materials that suit their specific needs or adapt existing learning materials to match their specific preferences. The goal of interventions based on student profile is to improve learning, retention and graduation rates (Baker et al., 2023; Brooks & Greer, 2014). Research also shows that predictive models can support educators by making complex data easier to interpret and act on. For example, results can be translated into learner personas – short, story-like descriptions of typical students – that help advisors and instructional designers plan targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring or online resources. These insights may also inform the redesign of teaching practices and guide how support services are allocated (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
Beyond individual classrooms, predictive modelling can also inform decision-making at the university level. When data from several courses or different cohorts of students are examined together, researchers may find patterns that may otherwise be missed. For instance, one empirical study found that biology students were most likely to drop out later in the course, around the sixth week of the semester (Gašević et al., 2016). In contrast, mathematics students tended to struggle earlier, probably because the material became more difficult sooner (Gašević et al., 2016). If the two cohorts were to be examined together without considering them as two different groups, the pattern would be more generic. Such pattern would capture the average, which here would describe neither biology students nor mathematics students. Specific findings like these can help teachers redesign courses, adjust teaching timelines, or add feedback opportunities at critical points. At the same time, administrators can use the information to decide where support services, such as tutoring or writing centers, are most needed (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Of course, predictions can be unfair when a model only has information that does not describe everyone correctly, for instance, if it only describes one behavior of one group of students. In these cases, the results of predictive modelling may be misleading. Predictions are also fairer when models are built with information that describes different groups of students. Using varied student information and checking the results regularly helps make predictions fairer (Kolkman, 2020). Accuracy of predictions also improves when models consider the course conditions in which students learn. Including factors such as whether a course design encourages self-regulated learning – so student behavior would include setting goals, monitoring progress, and reflecting on study strategies – produces results that are more meaningful and sensitive to educational course context (Gašević et al., 2016). In addition to supporting teachers and students, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress. When data from different studies are shared and compared, findings about student success and risk factors can be confirmed, building more reliable knowledge (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
At the institutional level, these models also contribute to ongoing improvement, as predictions are updated with new information from new students and used to guide decisions about courses and student support over time (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). It is important to acknowledge, however, that predictive modelling is not free of ethical risks. Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling is not just a technological tool. When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

Text B
Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).

To structure the key ideas from the two texts into a cohesive flow, you can follow the following guideline:

1. Introduction to Predictive Modelling in Education:
- Define predictive modelling as using past data to predict future behavior.

2. Benefits of Predictive Modelling in Education:

- Discuss the role of predictive modelling in improving course design, teaching practices, and support services allocation.

4. Challenges and Ethical Risks of Predictive Modelling in Education:
- Address the risks of reinforcing stereotypes and simplifying students based on demographic data.
- Highlight the limitations of predictive models when applied broadly and the importance of context in interpreting student behavior.
- Discuss the challenges of building models that are precise and meaningful in different educational contexts.

5. Accountability and Transparency in Predictive Modelling:
- Emphasize the importance of clarity in responsibility for unfair results produced by predictive models.
- Discuss the lack of theory and accountability in some predictive modelling studies.
- Mention the UK A-level grading case as an example of the potential harm caused by automated systems in education.

6. Conclusion:
- Summarize the advantages and ethical risks of predictive modelling in education.
- Stress the importance of using predictive modelling responsibly to avoid reinforcing inequalities and undermining trust in education.

By structuring the key ideas in this manner, you can create a coherent flow that transitions smoothly from discussing the benefits of predictive modelling to addressing the challenges and ethical considerations associated with its implementation in education.

Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).
llm_response:
Summary:
Predictive modeling in education involves using existing data to make predictions about future events, but it comes with risks. Models may inadvertently reinforce stereotypes and oversimplify students based on demographic data, potentially limiting their potential. Additionally, relying solely on online activity data to predict student behavior may not accurately reflect actual learning engagement. When applied institutionally, predictive models may not be universally applicable across different subjects, leading to misinterpretations and ineffective interventions. Challenges also arise from a lack of theoretical basis and accountability in how models are developed and used. The UK A-level grading case exemplifies how automated systems can produce unfair outcomes, highlighting the ethical concerns surrounding predictive modeling in education. Overall, while predictive modeling offers benefits, its ethical risks, potential for reinforcing inequalities, and lack of transparency and accountability underscore the importance of responsible and careful implementation in education.

In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

student_request:
Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).

student_request:
indicate the key points using bullet points in the following two texts:   Text A
Predictive modelling is based on the assumption that data that describes past events can be used to predict potential future behavior (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). In education, this technique has been commonly applied to identify students at risk of failure before they drop out of university. This is done by using past data of student behavior and drop-out to learn the relationship between how students behaved before they dropped out. This information about how students behaved is then used in models to predict drop-out from student behavior. This prediction takes place using new data, not the data from which the model inferred the relationship between student behavior and drop out has been learn from (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). Inferences from the data as described above make it possible for an early intervention before the student may be at risk.
Central to such interventions is the idea of a student profile, i.e. a data-based description of student characteristics, needs or preferences. Such a profile can be used to tailor student support to each specific student, such as recommend learning materials that suit their specific needs or adapt existing learning materials to match their specific preferences. The goal of interventions based on student profile is to improve learning, retention and graduation rates (Baker et al., 2023; Brooks & Greer, 2014). Research also shows that predictive models can support educators by making complex data easier to interpret and act on. For example, results can be translated into learner personas – short, story-like descriptions of typical students – that help advisors and instructional designers plan targeted interventions such as workshops, tutoring or online resources. These insights may also inform the redesign of teaching practices and guide how support services are allocated (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
Beyond individual classrooms, predictive modelling can also inform decision-making at the university level. When data from several courses or different cohorts of students are examined together, researchers may find patterns that may otherwise be missed. For instance, one empirical study found that biology students were most likely to drop out later in the course, around the sixth week of the semester (Gašević et al., 2016). In contrast, mathematics students tended to struggle earlier, probably because the material became more difficult sooner (Gašević et al., 2016). If the two cohorts were to be examined together without considering them as two different groups, the pattern would be more generic. Such pattern would capture the average, which here would describe neither biology students nor mathematics students. Specific findings like these can help teachers redesign courses, adjust teaching timelines, or add feedback opportunities at critical points. At the same time, administrators can use the information to decide where support services, such as tutoring or writing centers, are most needed (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
In the field of learning analytics, that is using educational data to improve teaching and learning, predictive modelling can make educational processes, such as student progress and participation, more transparent and easier for teachers to interpret (Gašević et al., 2016). Of course, predictions can be unfair when a model only has information that does not describe everyone correctly, for instance, if it only describes one behavior of one group of students. In these cases, the results of predictive modelling may be misleading. Predictions are also fairer when models are built with information that describes different groups of students. Using varied student information and checking the results regularly helps make predictions fairer (Kolkman, 2020). Accuracy of predictions also improves when models consider the course conditions in which students learn. Including factors such as whether a course design encourages self-regulated learning – so student behavior would include setting goals, monitoring progress, and reflecting on study strategies – produces results that are more meaningful and sensitive to educational course context (Gašević et al., 2016). In addition to supporting teachers and students, predictive modelling contributes to scientific progress. When data from different studies are shared and compared, findings about student success and risk factors can be confirmed, building more reliable knowledge (Brooks & Thompson, 2017).
Overall, predictive modelling can play an important role in making education more adaptive and responsive to the needs of students. When predictive models are applied carefully, they do not replace the judgment of educators but rather provide them with additional evidence to support their decisions (Brooks & Greer, 2014).
At the institutional level, these models also contribute to ongoing improvement, as predictions are updated with new information from new students and used to guide decisions about courses and student support over time (Brooks & Thompson, 2017). It is important to acknowledge, however, that predictive modelling is not free of ethical risks. Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling is not just a technological tool. When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

Text B
Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).

Models may reflect inequalities, or reduce student autonomy unless they are tested carefully, include information from many kinds of students, and are reviewed by outside experts (Baker et al., 2023; Kolkman, 2020). When developed responsibly, it can help make education fairer and more responsive to the needs of both students and teachers (Brooks & Greer, 2014).

When used carefully, these models provide educators with additional evidence to support their decisions without replacing their judgment. However, it is important to address ethical risks, such as reflecting inequalities or reducing student autonomy, by testing models carefully, including diverse student information, and seeking input from outside experts. When developed responsibly, predictive modelling can help create a fairer and more responsive educational system for both students and teachers.

student_request:
Take the following text, write a paragraph out of it. Use only the most important ideas : Predictive modelling means that existing data are used to make predictions about future events. However, when predicting models are applied in education, it carries certain risks. Instead of supporting student growth, models may classify learners in ways that reinforce stereotypes. For instance, models that rely heavily on demographic data such as race, gender or socioeconomic status can simplify students, making them feel defined by their background rather than their potential (Baker et al., 2023). In addition to student demographic data, many models predict future behavior by using student data from online activity, such as the number of logins, clicks on resources, or forum posts. However the meaning of these behaviours depends strongly on the context. The same action can signal success in one course but failure in another, because instructional design and course conditions shape how these tools are actually used (Gašević et al., 2016). Relying only on records of clicks or views gives a superficial picture of learning and may misclassify students by mistaking their surface activity of clicking around for a genuine effort to study and engage with the course content (Gašević et al, 2016).
At the institutional level, predictive models often face limits when applied too broadly. This is because predictors are not universal and do not have the same meaning in every subject, as the same feature can mean different things (Gašević et al., 2016). For example, an empirical study found that forum participation predicted achievement in a communications course, but in biology and mathematics it did not (Gašević et al., 2016). When institutions use the same model for all students, they risk misinterpreting these differences and designing interventions that do not address the real needs of learners and teachers. This tension shows the difficulty of building models that are easy to use across many courses, but also precise and meaningful in each context. As a result, institutional policies based only on combined data from different courses may not be very useful for making courses better or supporting teachers in improving their classes (Gašević et al., 2016).
Moreover, predictive modelling in education faces challenges when it is unclear how models work and they are not always based on how students actually learn. One major limitation is the lack of theory. Many studies rely mainly on online activity, and tend to overlook how learning actually happens in the classroom (Gašević et al., 2016). As a result, their findings cannot always be trusted and are often difficult to interpret.
Accountability is another concern. In other words, there needs to be clarity about who is responsible if a model produces unfair results. Testing whether results are fair and making small technical changes, while necessary, do not always prevent unfair outcomes. This was shown in the UK A-level grading case, where the algorithm followed formal rules but still produced results that harmed the most vulnerable students (Kolkman, 2020). These outcomes were only corrected after public debate and pressure (Kolkman. 2020). A final concern is that failures often remain hidden, particularly when they affect smaller or less visible groups. Without clear checks and reviews, these issues may go unnoticed and, over time, weaken the trust in education (Kolkman, 2020).
Overall, predictive modelling may offer some advantages, but its ethical risks are hard to ignore. Because models often rely on historical and demographic data, they can reproduce and even amplify existing inequalities (Baker et al., 2023). Collecting more data for prediction also creates concerns about privacy and about whether students are being monitored too closely (Baker & Thompson, 2017).
The UK A-level grading fiasco, in which an algorithm assigned grades based on students’ past school performance and school-level data, showed that automated systems that predict future based on past behavior can still produce unfair results, leaving students powerless to change the outcome (Kolkman, 2020). Without clear rules and responsibility, predictive modelling may undermine trust in education and widen the gap between students with advantages and those without (Gašević et al., 2016; Kolkman, 2020). In this sense, predictive modelling takes in the inequalities already present in society and risks reinforcing them in education, which shows why it must be used responsibly (Brooks & Thompson, 2017; Kolkman, 2020).
llm_response: Predictive modelling in education presents risks, as models may inadvertently reinforce stereotypes and oversimplify student classification based on demographic data. The interpretation of student behavior, such as online activity, can vary depending on the context of the course, leading to potential misclassifications. When applied broadly at the institutional level, predictive models may overlook subject-specific nuances, resulting in interventions that do not address the actual needs of learners and teachers. Challenges also arise from the lack of theoretical basis in some studies, leading to unreliable findings and difficulties in interpretation. Additionally, concerns about accountability, privacy, and the potential for unfair outcomes highlight the ethical considerations surrounding predictive modelling in education.
